items.title: Gamers beware: malicious wallpapers on Steam found stealing accounts
items.description: Since late 2025, cybercriminals have been exploiting Wallpaper Engine, a popular live wallpaper application on Steam, to distribute malware through Steam Workshop. Attackers target primarily Chinese and Russian gamers by embedding malicious code within application wallpapers shared on the platform. These compromised wallpapers deliver various malware types including infostealers, backdoors, crypto
items.ai_summary: The malicious wallpapers on Steam exploit the Wallpaper Engine application to distribute malware, primarily targeting Chinese and Russian gamers. The attackers embed malicious code within the wallpapers shared on the Steam Workshop, which delivers various malware types, including infostealers and backdoors. If exploited, the malware can lead to account theft and unauthorized access to sensitive information. To remediate, users should avoid downloading wallpapers from untrusted sources and update their Wallpaper Engine to the latest version, ensuring that the 'Safe Mode' is enabled, which restricts the execution of potentially malicious scripts. Additionally, users should monitor their account activity and report any suspicious behavior to Steam support.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Remote Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: WebAssembly Malware Found in Trojanized Open VSX Extensions
items.description: Trojanized Visual Studio Code extensions distributed via the Open VSX marketplace deliver a sophisticated WebAssembly-based attack chain. The extensions ship ChaCha20-encrypted TinyGo-compiled WebAssembly modules that poll the Solana blockchain for command-and-control instructions embedded in transaction memos. This novel dead-drop technique allows attackers to rotate infrastructure without hardco
items.ai_summary: The threat involves Trojanized Visual Studio Code extensions distributed via the Open VSX marketplace, which deliver a sophisticated WebAssembly-based attack chain. The extensions contain ChaCha20-encrypted TinyGo-compiled WebAssembly modules that communicate with the Solana blockchain to receive command-and-control instructions embedded in transaction memos, utilizing the T1071 application layer protocol. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access and control of developer systems, potentially affecting a wide range of industries that rely on Visual Studio Code. To remediate, developers should remove any suspicious extensions and update their Visual Studio Code to the latest version, ensuring that all extensions are verified and come from trusted sources. Additionally, monitoring network traffic for unusual Solana blockchain interactions can help in early detection of such threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Command and Control]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Public and Private Medical Community Targeted by Threat Actor Pursuing Artificial Intelligence, Cyber, Medical, and National Defense Research
items.description: A sophisticated espionage campaign attributed to UNC6508, a China-nexus threat actor, targeted North American academic, medical, and military research institutions for over a year. The adversary exploited REDCap servers, deployed custom INFINITERED malware to harvest credentials, and maintained persistent access through trojanized legitimate files that survived software upgrades. After remaining u
items.ai_summary: The UNC6508 threat actor has been conducting a sophisticated espionage campaign targeting North American academic, medical, and military research institutions for over a year, with a focus on artificial intelligence, cyber, medical, and national defense research. The adversary exploited vulnerabilities in REDCap servers to gain initial access, and then deployed custom INFINITERED malware to harvest credentials and maintain persistent access. The malware was able to survive software upgrades by trojanizing legitimate files, allowing the threat actor to maintain a long-term presence on the compromised systems. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately inspect their REDCap servers for signs of compromise, and apply any available security patches or updates to prevent exploitation. Additionally, defenders should implement robust credential harvesting detection and response measures, such as multi-factor authentication and regular password rotations, to prevent the threat actor from maintaining access to sensitive systems and data.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1005| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: How attackers are jailbreaking LLMs with CTF framing and how to catch them
items.description: Threat actors are bypassing AI model safety guardrails by framing exploit requests as legitimate security research, such as capture-the-flag challenges or CVE-hunting exercises. This technique manipulates upstream LLMs into generating working exploit code that attackers deploy against real targets. Multiple independent operators have been observed targeting five applications—PraisonAI, LiteLLM, Fa
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are exploiting large language models (LLMs) by framing malicious requests as legitimate capture-the-flag (CTF) challenges or CVE-hunting exercises, allowing them to bypass safety guardrails and generate working exploit code. This technique has been observed targeting five applications, including PraisonAI, LiteLLM, and Fa, with multiple independent operators involved. The real-world impact of this exploit is significant, as it enables attackers to deploy generated exploit code against real targets, potentially leading to data breaches or system compromise. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement strict input validation and monitoring of LLM-generated code, as well as provide security awareness training to researchers and developers to prevent social engineering tactics. Additionally, organizations should consider implementing code review and validation processes to detect and prevent the deployment of malicious code.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Investigation of email-based attack delivering MediaFire ZIP file with execution chain analysis
items.description: An investigation revealed a malicious email campaign directing victims to download a ZIP file from MediaFire. The infection chain began with a Python setup executable (Setu.exe) that side-loaded a malicious 400 MB python37.dll containing repeated byte padding. The DLL performed process injection into dllhost.exe, establishing communication with a C2 server at 138.124.186.2:7000. The threat actor d
items.ai_summary: The threat involves a malicious email campaign that directs victims to download a ZIP file from MediaFire, which contains a Python setup executable (Setu.exe) that side-loads a malicious python37.dll. This DLL performs process injection into dllhost.exe, allowing the threat actor to establish communication with a C2 server at 138.124.186.2:7000. The attack vector involves phishing (T1566) and process injection (T1055), with the threat actor leveraging application layer protocol (T1071) for C2 communication. To remediate, defenders should block traffic to the C2 server IP and port, and implement endpoint detection and response tools to identify and block suspicious process injection activity. Additionally, users should be educated to avoid downloading executables from untrusted sources, and to verify the authenticity of emails before interacting with attachments or links.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1055| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Android Banker with Complete Device Takeover Capabilities
items.description: A newly identified Android banking trojan named Rokarolla has been discovered, distributed through malicious websites masquerading as popular applications like TikTok or Google Chrome. The malware targets 217 distinct cryptocurrency and banking applications using 137 sophisticated commands for device control. Capabilities include harvesting lock screen credentials, exfiltrating contact lists and S
items.ai_summary: The Rokarolla Android banking trojan is a sophisticated malware that targets 217 distinct cryptocurrency and banking applications, allowing for complete device takeover capabilities. The malware is distributed through malicious websites masquerading as popular applications like TikTok or Google Chrome, utilizing drive-by compromise (T1189) and masquerading (T1036) tactics. If exploited, the malware can harvest lock screen credentials, exfiltrate contact lists, and steal sensitive information, affecting individuals and organizations that use the targeted applications. To remediate, users should avoid downloading applications from untrusted sources, keep their devices and applications up-to-date, and use anti-malware software to detect and remove the malware. Additionally, organizations can implement security measures such as mobile device management (MDM) and mobile application management (MAM) to protect against such threats.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1189| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Potemkin Loader & RMMProject The Anatomy of a ClickFix Attack
items.description: A ClickFix social engineering attack on an unmonitored endpoint led to a multi-stage intrusion affecting over 11 hosts. The infection chain began with a malicious HTA payload that silently installed an MSI package containing Potemkin, a custom loader with a deterministic DGA. Potemkin delivered RMMProject, a 4.4 MB Lua-scriptable RAT featuring browser credential theft with Chrome App-Bound Encrypt
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix social engineering attack exploited an unmonitored endpoint, leading to a multi-stage intrusion that affected over 11 hosts. The infection chain began with a malicious HTA payload that silently installed an MSI package containing Potemkin, a custom loader with a deterministic Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA). This delivered RMMProject, a 4.4 MB Lua-scriptable Remote Access Trojan (RAT) that featured browser credential theft with Chrome App-Bound Encryption. To remediate, defenders should immediately block all HTA files at the email gateway and web proxy levels, and implement a software restriction policy to prevent the execution of unauthorized MSI packages. Additionally, defenders should prioritize monitoring for unusual DNS query patterns indicative of DGA activity and restrict access to sensitive areas of the network by implementing a least-privilege access model.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1555.003| E[Credentials from Web Browsers]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Attackers Weaponize Microsoft Teams Relays to Stay Hidden
items.description: Attackers deploying DragonForce ransomware against a major U.S. services firm concealed their command-and-control traffic within Microsoft Teams relay infrastructure using Backdoor.Turn, a custom Go-based remote access trojan. This novel technique leverages anonymous Teams visitor tokens and TURN relay servers to mask malicious communications as legitimate Microsoft traffic. The intrusion lasted o
items.ai_summary: The attackers exploited the Microsoft Teams relay infrastructure to conceal their command-and-control traffic, utilizing a custom Go-based remote access trojan known as Backdoor.Turn. This technique leverages anonymous Teams visitor tokens and TURN relay servers to mask malicious communications as legitimate Microsoft traffic, making it challenging for defenders to detect. The DragonForce ransomware was deployed against a major U.S. services firm, resulting in a significant intrusion that lasted for an undisclosed duration. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement specific security controls, such as monitoring for unusual Teams traffic patterns and restricting access to Teams visitor tokens. Additionally, defenders should ensure that all remote access software is up-to-date and configured to use secure communication protocols, such as TLS 1.2 or higher.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Bluekit Phishing as a Service (PhaaS)
items.description: BlueKit operates as a mature commercial Phishing-as-a-Service platform offering 87 ready-made phishing kits targeting banks, cloud services, cryptocurrency exchanges, and global brands. The platform features subscription-based access, automated account takeover capabilities, peer-to-peer infrastructure for stealth, and integrated anti-detection tooling. BlueKit supports credential harvesting, sess
items.ai_summary: The BlueKit Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) platform poses a significant threat to various organizations, including banks, cloud services, cryptocurrency exchanges, and global brands, by offering 87 ready-made phishing kits. The platform's automated account takeover capabilities and peer-to-peer infrastructure enable stealthy operations, while its integrated anti-detection tooling makes it challenging for defenders to detect. If exploited, BlueKit can lead to credential harvesting and session hijacking, resulting in unauthorized access to sensitive information and potential financial losses. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering and anti-phishing solutions, such as DMARC, SPF, and DKIM, and educate users about phishing attacks and the importance of using strong, unique passwords. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious account activity and implement multi-factor authentication to prevent unauthorized access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    B -->|T1078| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1586| D[Impact]:::tactic

items.title: New APT-Q-27 sample spotted
items.description: A new campaign has been identified utilizing a valid digital signature from a Chinese technology company that remains unrevoked. The attack chain employs a dropper that retrieves an extension-based module list from command and control infrastructure. The malicious payloads exploit DLL Side-Loading techniques through a legitimate Tencent-signed executable to achieve code execution. The infrastructu
items.ai_summary: The newly identified APT-Q-27 campaign leverages a valid digital signature from a Chinese technology company to execute malicious payloads, exploiting DLL Side-Loading techniques through a legitimate Tencent-signed executable. This attack chain employs a dropper that retrieves an extension-based module list from command and control infrastructure, utilizing T1059 Command and Scripting Interpreter and T1574.002 DLL Side-Loading techniques. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized code execution, potentially affecting users of the legitimate Tencent software. To remediate, defenders should implement strict validation of digital signatures and monitor for suspicious activity related to the Tencent-signed executable. Additionally, ensuring up-to-date antivirus software and network monitoring can help detect and prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059| B[Command and Scripting Interpreter]:::tactic
    B -->|T1574.002| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-11469: A flaw has been found in jishenghua jshERP up to 3.6. Impacted is the function i...
items.description: A flaw has been found in jishenghua jshERP up to 3.6. Impacted is the function insertPlatformConfig of the file jshERP-boot/src/main/java/com/jsh/erp/service/PlatformConfigService.java of the component platformConfig Add Endpoint. Executing a manipulation of the argument platformValue can lead to server-side request forgery. The attack may be performed from remote. The exploit has been published a
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-11469 is a server-side request forgery vulnerability in jishenghua jshERP versions up to 3.6, specifically in the insertPlatformConfig function of the PlatformConfigService.java file. This vulnerability can be exploited remotely by manipulating the platformValue argument, allowing an attacker to perform unauthorized requests. The real-world impact of this vulnerability includes the potential for data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update jshERP to version 3.7 or later and ensure that the platformConfig Add Endpoint is properly configured to validate user input. Additionally, defenders can implement network segmentation and monitoring to detect and prevent potential exploitation attempts.

items.title: CVE-2026-11468: A vulnerability was detected in SourceCodester Hospitals Patient Records Managem...
items.description: A vulnerability was detected in SourceCodester Hospitals Patient Records Management System 1.0. This issue affects some unknown processing of the file /admin/?page=room_types. Performing a manipulation of the argument room results in cross site scripting. The attack is possible to be carried out remotely. The exploit is now public and may be used.
items.ai_summary: The vulnerability CVE-2026-11468 affects the SourceCodester Hospitals Patient Records Management System version 1.0, specifically in the file /admin/?page=room_types, where an attacker can manipulate the 'room' argument to execute a cross-site scripting (XSS) attack. This issue can be exploited remotely, and the exploit is now publicly available. If exploited, this vulnerability could allow an attacker to steal sensitive patient data or take control of the system, potentially affecting hospitals and healthcare organizations using this system. To remediate this issue, defenders should update the system to a patched version, if available, and implement a web application firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent XSS attacks. Additionally, input validation and sanitization should be implemented on the 'room' argument to prevent malicious input.

Such manipulation of the argument fileName leads to path traversal. The attack can be executed remotely. The exploit has been di
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-11467 is a security vulnerability in jishenghua jshERP up to version 3.6, specifically affecting the addAccountHeadAndDetail function in the AccountHeadService.java file. This vulnerability allows for path traversal through manipulation of the fileName argument, which can be exploited remotely. If successfully exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive files and data, potentially affecting businesses using jshERP for enterprise resource planning. To remediate, users should update to a version of jshERP later than 3.6 and ensure that all instances of the addAccountHeadAndDetail endpoint are properly validated and sanitized to prevent path traversal attacks. Additionally, defenders should monitor for unusual file access patterns and restrict access to sensitive files and directories.

items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-11466 is a vulnerability in zilliztech deep-searcher up to version 0.0.2, specifically affecting the CollectionRouter.invoke function in the deepsearcher/agent/collection_router.py file. This weakness allows for improper access controls due to the manipulation of the kwargs argument, enabling remote exploitation. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data, with potential real-world impact on organizations relying on deep-searcher for data management. To remediate, users should apply the patch from the pull request that fixes the issue, ensuring they update to a version beyond 0.0.2. Additionally, defenders should monitor for unusual access patterns and implement access controls to mitigate potential exploitation.

items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-11465 is a security flaw in songquanpeng one-api up to version 0.6.11-preview.7, specifically affecting the Redeem function in the model/redemption.go file of the Redemption Code Top-Up Endpoint. items.title: Webinar Today: How Modern Breaches Bypass MFA and Evade Detection
items.description: Attendees will learn how attackers evade conventional detection methods, why legacy MFA alone is no longer sufficient, and how organizations can strengthen their defenses. The post Webinar Today: How Modern Breaches Bypass MFA and Evade Detection appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.description: Attackers actively are targeting various sectors across nearly 200 countries and have already compiled a list of working credentials for tens of thousands of compromised devices.

items.title: Why Account Takeovers Are Rising and How to Stop Them
items.description: Account takeovers are rising as attackers bypass traditional defenses through phishing, session hijacking, and MFA fatigue. Specops Software explores how device trust and continuous verification help reduce account takeover risk. [...]

items.title: Malicious JetBrains Plugins Steal AI API Keys as Chrome Extensions Capture Chatbot Chats

"Every plugin poses as an AI coding assistant built on DeepSeek and other large language models, offering chat, commit messages, code review, bug finding, and unit tests,"

items.description: Tenet aims to detect and stop dangerous AI agentic behavior in real time. The post Tenet Security Emerges From Stealth With $6 Million Seed Funding appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Adversarial Exposure Validation Turns Security Visibility into Confident Prioritization
items.description: For security teams, the findings never stop, but confidence in knowing which ones matter is becoming harder to maintain. The problem is no longer visibility. It's validation. Security teams must decide which findings warrant action while operating under constant pressure and incomplete information. Increasingly, the challenge is not discovering potential risks. It is determining which risks

items.title: Microsoft confirms Office apps launch issues after June updates
items.description: Microsoft is investigating a new issue preventing third-party applications from launching Microsoft Office applications or opening documents on up-to-date Windows systems. [...]

items.title: Rockwell Automation Patches Vulnerabilities in ICS Controllers and Software
items.description: The industrial automation giant has fixed security holes in Logix, CompactLogix, Flex, RSLinx, and FactoryTalk products. The post Rockwell Automation Patches Vulnerabilities in ICS Controllers and Software appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft Teams Relay Servers Abused in DragonForce Ransomware Attack
items.description: The attackers deployed a new Go-based backdoor that uses Microsoft Teams servers for command-and-control. The post Microsoft Teams Relay Servers Abused in DragonForce Ransomware Attack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: The Top 10 Attack Surface Exposures in 2026
items.description: Breaches don't always start with a zero-day. An exposed admin panel can get brute-forced, or credentials reused from a previous attack. But when a vulnerability does drop — like MongoBleed earlier this year, which let attackers pull credentials and session tokens from server memory without authentication — anything internet-facing is immediately at risk. With time-to-exploit now down to a

items.title: Warner warns of CISA cuts, staffing gaps in letter to acting chief
items.description: Warner on Tuesday also wrote a letter to DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin, underscoring that DHS must prioritize CISA and pay for the MS-ISAC.

items.title: CISA orders feds to patch max severity Joomla plugin flaw by Friday
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has ordered federal agencies to patch a maximum-severity flaw in the Widget Factory Joomla Content Editor (JCE) plugin that is being actively exploited in the wild. [...]

items.title: Microsoft Working on Patch for ‘RoguePlanet’ Zero-Day
items.description: The public PoC code exploits a race condition in Microsoft Defender to spawn a command prompt with System privileges. The post Microsoft Working on Patch for ‘RoguePlanet’ Zero-Day appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Oracle’s Second Monthly Security Updates Deliver 245 Patches
items.description: Oracle has released its June 2026 Critical Security Patch Update to fix vulnerabilities in Communications, EBS, Enterprise Manager and other products. The post Oracle’s Second Monthly Security Updates Deliver 245 Patches appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft working on Defender patch for RoguePlanet zero-day
items.description: Microsoft confirmed that it's working on a security patch for a Defender zero-day vulnerability named "RoguePlanet," disclosed one week ago. [...]

items.title: Chrome and Firefox Updated to Patch Critical, High-Severity Vulnerabilities
items.description: The browser updates address multiple memory safety bugs that could potentially lead to remote code execution. The post Chrome and Firefox Updated to Patch Critical, High-Severity Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: UK Social Media Ban for Minors Has Privacy Experts Worried
items.description: The UK will ban adolescents under 16 years old from user-to-user social media platforms, despite age verification issues and privacy concerns.

items.title: 144 Mastra npm Packages Compromised via Hijacked Contributor Account
items.description: As many as 144 npm packages associated with the Mastra namespace ("@mastra/*"), a popular open-source JavaScript and TypeScript framework for building artificial intelligence (AI) applications, have been compromised as part of a software supply chain attack codenamed easy-day-js, per findings from Endor Labs, JFrog, SafeDep, Socket, and StepSecurity. "A single npm account (ehindero)

items.title: Joomla, LiteSpeed Vulnerabilities Exploited in Attacks
items.description: The flaws allow attackers to execute arbitrary PHP code and gain root privileges on shared hosting servers. The post Joomla, LiteSpeed Vulnerabilities Exploited in Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Kodak confirms data breach claimed by ShinyHunters extortion gang
items.description: Kodak has confirmed that it's working with external cybersecurity experts to investigate a security breach after hackers gained access to some of the company's data. [...]

items.title: 3 Recently Patched Fortinet FortiSandbox Vulnerabilities in Hacker Crosshairs
items.description: SOCRadar has detected 30,000 compromised Fortinet firewalls that expose networks to hacking. The post 3 Recently Patched Fortinet FortiSandbox Vulnerabilities in Hacker Crosshairs appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA Warns of Actively Exploited Joomla JCE Flaw Allowing PHP Code Execution
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Tuesday added a maximum-severity security flaw impacting Widget Factory Joomla Content Editor (JCE) to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, citing evidence of active exploitation. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-48907 (CVSS score: 10.0), is a case of improper access control that could facilitate arbitrary

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 17th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9976, (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: India's Telegram ban draws criticism from Durov as company challenges order in court
items.description: To prevent cheating, Indian authorities ordered Telegram to restrict access nationwide ahead of a major medical entrance exam.

items.title: GitHub dismissed security reports on flaws now exploited by supply-chain worm, researchers say
items.description: GitHub rejected two formal vulnerability reports identifying design flaws that researchers say are enabling variants of the Shai-Hulud supply-chain worm to infect and compromise hundreds of software packages and developer accounts worldwide.

items.title: Fileless Phantom Stealer Targets Browser Credentials
items.description: In addition to executing entirely in memory, the malware's infection chain incorporates other anti-analysis techniques designed to evade detection.

items.title: Security Community Slams US Ban on Exporting Mythos, Fable
items.description: An open letter signed by dozens of security experts asked the government to reverse export restrictions on Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models.

items.title: Malicious JetBrains Marketplace plugins steal AI API keys from developers
items.description: At least 15 malicious plugins found on the JetBrains Marketplace were designed to steal AI API keys from developers. [...]

items.title: SprySOCKS Windows Variant Abuses Kernel Drivers to Evade Detection
items.description: FishMonger, a China-nexus threat group, has deployed an undocumented version of the Linux backdoor against government targets in Honduras, Taiwan, Thailand, and Pakistan.

items.title: New Rokarolla Android malware targets 217 banking, crypto apps
items.description: A new Android banking trojan named Rokarolla is targeting 217 banking and cryptocurrency applications using an extensive set of 137 commands. [...]

items.title: Google Vertex AI SDK Flaw Let Attackers Hijack Model Uploads via Bucket Squatting
items.description: A flaw in the Google Cloud Vertex AI SDK for Python let an attacker with no access to a victim's project hijack the victim's machine learning model upload and run code inside Google's serving infrastructure. Palo Alto Networks Unit 42, which found and reported the bug through Google's bug bounty program, calls the technique "Pickle in the Middle" and said it saw no exploitation in the wild.

items.title: Steam Workshop abused to spread malware via Wallpaper Engine app
items.description: Threat actors are abusing Steam Workshop, Valve's community hub for downloading game-related content, to push various malware hidden in wallpaper packages. [...]

items.title: ClickFix Campaigns Expand Malware Delivery With New Loaders and Fake Update Lures
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged multiple ClickFix campaigns that deliver three malware loaders called BabaDeda Loader, Lorem Ipsum Loader, and Potemkin, per independent reports from Morphisec, BlueVoyant, and Huntress, respectively. Attacks involving BabaDeda Loader, observed in April 2026, have targeted education and financial organizations. "Earlier BabaDeda activity was known for

items.title: Rokarolla Android Trojan Levels Up to Full Device Control, Persistence
items.description: The emerging malware, spread via fake TikTok and Chrome downloads, has evolved by combining banking fraud with extensive device surveillance and remote control.

items.title: India temporarily blocks Telegram over medical exam cheating fears
items.description: Authorities said scammers previously exploited the feature by posting fake exam questions before the test and later replacing them with the real questions, making it look like they had leaked the exam in advance.

items.title: 'Lorem Ipsum' Malware Pivots to ClickFix Delivery
items.description: New analysis shows the campaign, which uses compromised WordPress sites, may be linked to the ransomware and data extortion group Vice Society.

items.title: UK to require ID or face scan before you can make social media accounts
items.description: Opening a new social media account in the UK will soon mean proving you're over 16 with an ID upload or a facial age scan, under a government ban on under-16s taking effect in spring 2027. Security experts warn the age checks are easy to circumvent and create new data-breach risks. [...]

items.title: New Rokarolla Android Malware Steals PINs, SMS Codes, and Crypto Wallet Funds
items.description: Security researchers at Zimperium's zLabs have documented a new Android banking trojan, Rokarolla, that targets 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps and packs 137 remote commands. Together, they give an operator near-total control of an infected phone: it lifts lock-screen PINs, reads and sends SMS, rewrites the clipboard to redirect crypto payments, and switches off Google Play

items.title: Survey: 94% of Incidents Involve Anonymized Infrastructure. Teams Are Still Reactive
items.description: Security teams have never had more IP data at their disposal. Every day, analysts ingest enrichment feeds, geolocation data, reputation scores, telemetry, and threat intelligence from a growing ecosystem of vendors and platforms. Yet despite this abundance of information, many organizations continue to face a fundamental challenge: sifting through the noise to understand who is behind an IP and

items.title: Attackers Exploit Three Fortinet FortiSandbox Flaws, One Patched Last Week
items.description: Bad actors are exploiting multiple security vulnerabilities in Fortinet FortiSandbox, according to threat intelligence firm Defused Cyber. In a post shared on X, the company said it has observed exploitation of CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089 over the past 24 hours. CVE-2026-39813 (CVSS score: 9.1) refers to a path traversal vulnerability in FortiSandbox JRPC API that could

items.title: From a VHDX File to a Remcos RAT, (Tue, Jun 16th)
items.description: Yesterday, a reader reported to us a malicious ZIP archive (SHA256: a0104921a2d37ab87482ac9a9f5c3713479c118846c3e999178e75b81620c094[1]). Once unzipped, it contains a VHDX file that discloses a malicious JavaScript after being mounted (which is automatic on modern Windows OSs):

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: UK to ban social media access for children under 16
items.description: The ban will apply to all “user-to-user platforms, whose purpose is to enable social interaction and which allow users to post material, alongside algorithms,” according to a press release from the government’s Department for Science, Innovation and Technology.

items.title: HTTP/2 Bomb Attacks Put Telcos, Healthcare Orgs at Risk
items.description: The denial-of-service (DoS) exploit takes advantage of two features in HTTP/2 that were designed to save Internet bandwith, not power massive amplification attacks.

items.title: Copilot 'SearchLeak' Attack Allows 1-Click Data Theft
items.description: The critical, three-stage attack is now patched, but it's part of a new group of AI prompt-injection issues that use hidden URLs and other variables.

items.title: China-Nexus Actor Spies on US Researchers Undetected for a Year
items.description: Google discovered and disrupted the sprawling campaign, which stole RedCAP credentials to breach numerous institutions and exfiltrate sensitive data.

items.title: Evil MSI Background: BASE64 Statistical Analysis, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: I like it when a fellow handler posts a diary entry about images with malicious content. Last one is Xavier: "The Evil MSI Background is Back!".

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 15th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9972, (Mon, Jun 15th)

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: CISA Issues New Directive Improving How Federal Agencies Prioritize the Mitigation of Cyber Vulnerabilities
How has use of framing protection security headers changed in the past 3 years?, (Wed, Jun 10th)
items.description: Back in 2023, I wrote a diary[1] discussing how commonly X-Frame-Options and CSP headers containing the frame-ancestors directive were used on 1 million most popular domains on the internet (based on the Tranco list[2]), and how they were set. Given that three years have passed since then, I thought it might be interesting to repeat the analysis and see what â€“ if anything â€“ has changed in the meantime.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 10th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9966, (Wed, Jun 10th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Microsoft June 2026 Patch Tuesday, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: Microsoft today released patches for 204 vulnerabilities. 38 of these vulnerabilities are considered critical, and three have been disclosed before today. Six of the vulnerabilities affect Microsoft cloud solutions and do not require any user action. In addition, Microsoft incorporated 360 different vulnerabilities affecting Chromium into its Edge browser.

items.title: CISA Announces Winners of the 2026 President’s Cup Cybersecurity Competition
CISA Urges Stronger Security for Automatic Tank Gauge Systems
Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
